period_summary: Psalm 110:1 is among the most frequently cited OT texts in patristic literature, concentrated heavily from the mid-2nd through 4th centuries, revealing its central role in Christological argumentation and the universal dominance of the LXX text form in Christian apologetic and doctrinal contexts.

citations.cited_text: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου, ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου
citations.text_form_note: Justin's citation precisely matches the LXX rendering with 'Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου' and 'ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου', differing from the MT's 'לִימִינִי' (at my right hand) which LXX renders with ἐκ δεξιῶν. The use of κύριος for both YHWH and Adonai follows standard LXX practice.
citations.theological_use: Justin deploys this text as a central proof of Christ's divinity and exaltation, arguing that the two 'lords' demonstrate the Father addressing the pre-existent Son. The 'sitting at the right hand' becomes evidence of Christ's current heavenly session and eschatological victory over enemies.
citations.transmission_implication: Justin's repeated LXX-form citations indicate that by mid-2nd century Rome, the LXX was the authoritative scriptural text for Christian apologetic purposes.

citations.cited_text: Dixit Dominus Domino meo: Sede a dextris meis, donec ponam inimicos tuos scabellum pedum tuorum
citations.text_form_note: Though surviving in Latin translation, Irenaeus's citation reflects the LXX pattern ('sede a dextris meis' = κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου; 'scabellum pedum tuorum' = ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου). The Old Latin rendering faithfully represents the Greek Vorlage he used.
citations.theological_use: Irenaeus employs the verse to establish the distinction between Father and Son against Gnostic Christologies, while simultaneously affirming the Son's share in divine authority. The 'sitting' language proves Christ's post-ascension sovereignty.
citations.transmission_implication: The Latin transmission via Old Latin versions preserves an underlying Greek LXX text form, indicating LXX dominance extended to Western Christian communities by the late 2nd century.

citations.cited_text: Dixit Dominus Domino meo: Sede a dextris meis, quoadusque ponam inimicos tuos scabellum pedum tuorum
citations.text_form_note: Tertullian's Latin citation follows the LXX tradition through Old Latin intermediaries ('sede a dextris' for ἐκ δεξιῶν, 'scabellum pedum' for ὑποπόδιον ποδῶν). The 'quoadusque' (until) reflects ἕως ἄν of the LXX.
citations.theological_use: Tertullian utilizes the text polemically against Marcion to demonstrate that the Creator God of the OT is the Father of Christ, with the two 'Dominus' figures proving the Father-Son distinction within monotheism. The eschatological subjection of enemies affirms the continuity of divine plan from OT to NT.
citations.transmission_implication: North African Latin Christianity's reliance on LXX-based Old Latin texts confirms the geographical breadth of LXX-form transmission in pre-Vulgate Western churches.

citations.cited_text: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου, ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου
citations.text_form_note: Origen's citation consistently follows the LXX in all his extant works. Despite his knowledge of Hebrew (evidenced in the Hexapla), he cites the LXX form for theological exposition, including the characteristic LXX renderings ἐκ δεξιῶν and ὑποπόδιον.
citations.theological_use: Origen interprets the psalm allegorically and typologically, seeing Christ's session at the right hand as both historical (post-ascension) and mystical (the soul's ascent). The subjection of enemies represents the defeat of demonic powers and the ultimate reconciliation of all things.
citations.transmission_implication: Even the most textually sophisticated father of the 3rd century, with access to Hebrew and multiple Greek recensions, defaulted to the LXX for homiletical and exegetical purposes.

citations.cited_text: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου, ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου
citations.text_form_note: Eusebius's citation is verbatim LXX, reflecting the standard Greek text of his era. His access to Origen's Hexapla and library at Caesarea makes his consistent use of the LXX form particularly significant, as he could have noted variant readings but chose not to.
citations.theological_use: Eusebius employs the verse as a primary proof-text demonstrating prophecy fulfillment, linking it to Jesus's own citation in the Gospels (Matt 22:44). The 'sitting' motif proves Christ's divine nature and eternal priesthood after the order of Melchizedek (Ps 110:4).
citations.transmission_implication: Eusebius's citations from the premier scholarly library of early Christianity confirm the LXX as the stable, authoritative text form, with no evidence of competing Greek traditions for this verse.

citations.cited_text: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου
citations.text_form_note: Athanasius quotes the LXX form precisely, particularly emphasizing the 'ἐκ δεξιῶν μου' construction. His extensive theological commentary on the 'sitting' language depends on the LXX rendering rather than any alternative text form.
citations.theological_use: This text is central to Athanasius's anti-Arian polemic, proving that the Son shares the Father's throne and divine honors while remaining distinct in person. The 'sitting at the right hand' refutes Arian subordinationism by demonstrating equality of honor and power.
citations.transmission_implication: 4th-century Alexandrian orthodoxy's foundational Christological arguments presuppose the LXX text form, indicating no serious textual plurality in Greek-speaking Christianity.

citations.cited_text: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου· Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου, ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου
citations.text_form_note: Chrysostom's citation matches the standard LXX text exactly, reflecting the Antiochene liturgical and exegetical tradition. His homiletical exposition depends on the precise LXX wording, particularly the ἐκ δεξιῶν construction and the future subjunctive θῶ.
citations.theological_use: Chrysostom uses the passage to expound Christ's human and divine natures, arguing that 'sitting' refers to the exalted humanity of Christ sharing the Father's throne. The eschatological victory over enemies encourages persecuted Christians with assurance of ultimate triumph.
citations.transmission_implication: Late 4th-century Antiochene Christianity, despite its emphasis on literal-historical exegesis and contact with Syriac traditions, exclusively used the LXX form for this heavily-cited psalm.

citations.cited_text: Dixit Dominus Domino meo: Sede a dextris meis, donec ponam inimicos tuos scabellum pedum tuorum
citations.text_form_note: Augustine's Latin citation reflects the Old Latin (Vetus Latina) tradition based on the LXX, with 'sede a dextris meis' and 'scabellum pedum tuorum' representing the standard Greek readings. Though aware of Jerome's Vulgate revisions toward Hebrew, Augustine prefers the traditional Old Latin.
citations.theological_use: Augustine interprets the psalm ecclesiologically and Christologically: Christ is both speaker and subject, head of the body (Church). The 'sitting' represents Christ's rest after the work of redemption, while the subjection of enemies unfolds progressively through Church history.
citations.transmission_implication: Early 5th-century Latin Africa's preference for Old Latin over Jerome's Hebraica veritas demonstrates the entrenched authority of the LXX tradition transmitted through Latin, resisting revision toward proto-MT.

transmission_synthesis: The patristic citation evidence for Psalm 110:1 reveals unanimous use of the LXX text form across all geographical regions (Rome, Gaul, Carthage, Alexandria, Caesarea, Antioch, Constantinople, North Africa) and throughout the entire period (2nd–5th centuries). No father cited a text form closer to the MT or attested any significant textual variants; all citations consistently preserve the LXX's distinctive renderings: ἐκ δεξιῶν μου (at my right hand), κάθου (sit), and ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου (footstool of your feet). This uniformity, despite the passage's extremely high citation frequency and its use in diverse theological contexts (apologetic, anti-heretical, homiletical, polemical), strongly suggests a stable, singular textual tradition within Christianity from its earliest period. Even textually sophisticated fathers like Origen and Eusebius, with access to Hebrew and multiple recensions, consistently cited the LXX form, indicating it was regarded as the authoritative Christian scripture. The Latin fathers (Irenaeus, Tertullian, Augustine) transmitted the LXX through Old Latin versions, and Augustine's resistance to Jerome's Hebrew-based revisions demonstrates the LXX's entrenched status even when alternative text forms became available.
transmission_synthesis_plain: Early Christian writers from the 2nd through 5th centuries, whether writing in Greek or Latin and regardless of their location across the Roman Empire, all cited Psalm 110:1 in exactly the same form—the Greek translation (Septuagint) rather than the Hebrew. This remarkable unanimity, for one of the most frequently quoted Old Testament verses, shows that Christians used a single, stable text of this psalm for nearly four centuries. Even scholars who knew Hebrew preferred the Greek version for teaching and preaching, indicating it was considered the Church's authoritative Bible.

notable_variants.reading: Εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου (The Lord said to my Lord)
notable_variants.significance: The LXX's rendering of both YHWH and Adonai as κύριος enabled Christian Christological interpretation of 'two Lords' as Father and Son, which became foundational for Trinitarian theology. The MT's use of two different Hebrew terms (YHWH and Adoni) creates less obvious support for this reading, making the LXX form theologically consequential for patristic exegesis.

notable_variants.reading: Κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου (Sit at my right hand)
notable_variants.significance: The LXX's ἐκ δεξιῶν (from/at the right) differs subtly from the MT's לִימִינִי (at my right hand), with the Greek preposition ἐκ allowing interpretation of spatial location and origin, which patristic writers exploited for theological purposes regarding Christ's procession from the Father and shared authority.

notable_variants.reading: ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου (footstool of your feet)
notable_variants.significance: The LXX's fuller expression 'footstool of your feet' (versus MT's simple 'footstool for your feet') provided richer imagery for patristic exposition of Christ's eschatological victory, linking to other 'footstool' texts (Isa 66:1, Matt 5:35) in theological arguments about Christ's cosmic sovereignty.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Universal LXX Dominance in Christianity's Most-Cited Psalm
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The complete absence of any MT-aligned or variant text forms for Psalm 110:1 across 300+ years of patristic citations, despite the verse's extraordinarily high frequency and theological centrality, reveals that the LXX achieved exclusive scriptural authority in Christianity at a very early stage—likely before 150 CE. The fact that even the Hexapla's compiler (Origen) and Latin revisers aware of Hebraica veritas (Augustine's contemporaries) never cite alternative forms suggests the LXX text was not merely preferred but functionally canonical. This uniformity implies that Psalm 110:1's LXX form was already fixed in Christian liturgical, catechetical, and apologetic tradition by the time of our earliest patristic witnesses, making textual revision theologically and practically impossible. The text's stability served Christological dogma: any deviation would have undermined centuries of accumulated theological interpretation.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Psalm 110:1 is probably the most-quoted Old Testament verse in early Christianity, yet every single Church Father cited it in exactly the same Greek translation form. This tells us that Christians treated the Greek Septuagint as their unchangeable scripture very early, and the wording became so theologically important for proving Christ's divinity that no one would consider using a different version.
